title: Explosive-Device Safety: Static Electricity, Grounding, and Clothing
narrationScript: Neutron radiography sees hydrogen, so much of its work is live ordnance — detonators, explosive bolts, and cartridge-actuated devices imaged right through their metal housings. The moment one enters your cell, accidental initiation joins radiation on the hazard list, and the ignition source you generate personally is static electricity. Ordinary movement charges your body to thousands of volts, dry air multiplies the numbers, and you feel nothing below about three thousand volts — while the most sensitive initiators can fire from far less. The defense is potential control. Bonding connects objects to each other; grounding drains the whole system to earth. Conductive floors, grounded bench tops, a wrist strap with its one-megohm resistor, daily-tested footwear, and shunted initiator leads keep everything at one potential, and you prove every path before you touch. Dress as part of the system: cotton outer garments, no synthetics, no changing clothes in the area. And always the cardinal principle — minimum people, minimum explosives, minimum time.

sections.heading: Why Explosive Devices End Up in a Neutron Beam
sections.body:
Thermal neutrons see the world in reverse from X-rays and gamma rays: they pass relatively freely through most structural metals, then stop hard in **hydrogen-rich material**. Explosives, propellants, and pyrotechnics are exactly that — hydrogenous organic compounds — so neutron radiography can image the **explosive fill inside a sealed metal housing**: detonators, explosive bolts, cutting cord, cartridge- and propellant-actuated devices for ejection seats, rocket igniters, and flight-termination ordnance. The inspection looks for missing fill, voids, gaps, cracked or shifted explosive columns, and foreign material — flaws that decide whether a one-shot device fires when a life depends on it.

That capability carries a price: the radiographer becomes part of an **explosives operation**. The items most often submitted for neutron radiography include the most sensitive members of the energetic-materials family — **initiators and detonators** built around static-sensitive primary explosives, many of them fired electrically through a fine **bridgewire**. Accidental initiation joins radiation on your hazard list, and the ignition source you personally generate all day long is **static electricity**.

Every explosives operation runs on the **cardinal principle**: expose the **minimum number of people** to the **minimum quantity of explosives** for the **minimum amount of time**. Only trained, authorized personnel handle devices, the quantity allowed in the cell is limited, and a written procedure scripts every step.

sections.heading: Static Electricity: Charge You Make by Moving
sections.body:
Static charge comes from the **triboelectric effect**: when two dissimilar materials touch and then separate, electrons transfer, leaving one surface positive and the other negative. No machinery is required — **you** are the generator. Walking across a floor, sliding off a chair, pulling off a jacket, unrolling tape or plastic film — all of it pumps charge onto your body, where it stays stored, because the human body is a capacitor of roughly **100 to 500 picofarads**.

Two facts decide everything about this hazard:

- **Generation is huge.** On a dry day, ordinary walking can charge you to **tens of thousands of volts** — handbook values run as high as 35,000 V for walking across carpet at 10–25 percent relative humidity.
- **Perception is terrible.** Most people cannot feel a discharge below about **3,000 volts**. Below that, you spark with no sensation at all — and even a discharge at the threshold you can barely feel carries energy on the order of **a millijoule or more**, while the most sensitive primary explosives and electroexplosive devices can be initiated by **fractions of a millijoule**.

**Humidity** is the silent variable. Moist air leaves a thin conductive film on surfaces that bleeds charge away nearly as fast as you make it: the same walk that generates 35,000 V in dry air may generate only 1,500 V at 65–90 percent relative humidity. That is why many procedures for exposed static-sensitive explosives require relative humidity around **50 percent or higher** — and why winter air, air conditioning, and desert sites all raise the threat.

sections.caption: Typical electrostatic voltages generated by ordinary movement in dry and humid air, plotted against the roughly 3,000-volt threshold below which a person feels nothing.

sections.title: A spark you cannot feel can still fire a detonator
sections.body: The most sensitive initiators and primary explosives can ignite at energies far below the smallest discharge a human can perceive. Comfort tells you nothing. If you have not bonded, grounded, verified, and dressed for the work, assume your body is charged — and never touch an explosive device, or anything connected to one, until you and it are at the same proven potential.

sections.heading: Grounding and Bonding: Hold Everything at One Potential
sections.body:
A spark needs a **potential difference**. Erase the difference and no spark is possible — that is the entire strategy, executed with two distinct connections:

- **Bonding** connects conductive objects **to each other**, eliminating potential differences *between them*. Bonded objects may still carry charge — but they are charged together, so nothing jumps the gap.
- **Grounding** connects the bonded system **to earth**, draining the charge away and holding everything at earth potential.

The hardware in an explosive-device radiography cell builds one continuous, verified path:

- A facility **earth electrode and ground bus** that every other connection terminates on.
- **Conductive or static-dissipative flooring** bonded into the ground system.
- **Grounded conductive work surfaces** — never an insulating mat under a sensitive device.
- A **personnel wrist strap** with a built-in series resistance of about **1 megohm** that drains your body continuously. The resistor is deliberate: it limits the current through you if you accidentally contact energized equipment, while still bleeding static off in a fraction of a second.
- **Conductive footwear** working with the floor as your second, hands-free drain path.

Resistance everywhere follows the same logic — a window, not a race to zero: **low enough to drain charge** faster than you generate it, **high enough to protect you** from electrical-fault current. Typical floor specifications run from about 25,000 ohms up to 1 megohm to ground; your facility standard states the binding values.

sections.caption: A grounding and bonding arrangement for explosive-device radiography: earth electrode and ground bus, conductive floor, grounded work surface, wrist strap with series resistor, tested footwear, shunted initiator, and a bonding jumper to the transport container.

sections.heading: Shunts, Floating Conductors, and Proving the Path
sections.body:
Three habits separate a disciplined cell from a lucky one.

**Keep initiators shunted.** Electroexplosive devices arrive with their lead wires **twisted together and shorted by a shunt** — a shorting clip or cap. The shunt holds both leads at the same potential, so a stray discharge cannot drive current *through* the bridgewire. It stays in place until a written procedure step expressly removes it. A device that arrives with its shunt missing is a stop-work condition, not an inconvenience — hold everything and call for direction.

**Hunt floating conductors.** An ungrounded metal object — a tray sitting on a rubber mat, a foil wrapper, an unbonded cart, a wristwatch — picks up charge by contact or induction and waits to deliver all of it in one spark. Every conductive item near the device gets bonded or gets removed. The same goes for charge generators: poly bags, bubble wrap, plastic tape, and synthetic wipes stay out of the cell.

**Prove the path, then touch.** Wrist straps and footwear are tested **daily, before explosives work** — on a tester, not by faith. Floor and bench resistance to ground is measured on schedule and recorded. Bonding jumpers go on **before** a container is opened or a device is transferred, and you ground yourself **before** your hand reaches the device. The sequence never changes: **bond, ground, verify — then touch.**

sections.heading: Clothing Requirements for Explosive-Device Work
sections.body:
Your clothing is part of the static-control system, and the rules are specific:

- **Outer garments are cotton** or another approved static-dissipative fabric, reasonably snug-fitting. Synthetics — nylon, polyester, acetate — along with silk and wool are strong triboelectric generators and are prohibited as outer layers.
- **Never put on or remove outer clothing inside the explosive area.** Pulling off a sweater is one of the best static generators ever devised — thousands of volts in one motion. Change layers outside, then re-verify your grounds.
- **Footwear is conductive or static-dissipative**, compatible with the floor system, and **tested every day** before explosives work begins.
- **Gloves, where required, are cotton or an approved dissipative type.** Ordinary rubber or latex gloves insulate your hands — isolating you from your ground path while generating charge — and are prohibited unless your procedure specifically allows them.
- **Personal items stay out**: rings, watches, and other metal jewelry are ungrounded conductors riding on your body, and matches, lighters, and other spark- or flame-producing devices are excluded from any explosive area.
- Where a **flash or fire hazard** exists, the procedure adds flame-resistant outer garments.

These are typical requirements drawn from explosives-safety standards. Your facility's written procedure states the binding version — and it wins every tie.

sections.caption: Explosive-area clothing and personal-equipment rules at a glance.
rows: Cotton or approved static-dissipative fabric; no synthetic outer layers
Nylon and polyester generate thousands of volts with ordinary movement
Removing a garment is itself a high-voltage static generator
Conductive or static-dissipative, tested daily with the floor system
Completes the continuous person-to-ground drain path
About 1 megohm series resistance; tested before use
Drains body charge continuously while limiting fault current through you
Cotton or approved dissipative types only
Rubber and latex insulate you from ground and generate charge
Rings, watches, matches, and lighters stay out
Ungrounded conductors and ignition sources have no place near primary explosives

figures.title: Static voltages from ordinary movement versus the human perception threshold
Grounding and bonding arrangement for explosive-device radiography

interactive.title: Static Hazard or Static Control?
interactive.intro: An initiator is on its way to your neutron radiography cell. Sort each item: does it build or invite static charge — keep it out or fix it — or does it drain and equalize charge as a required control?
buckets: Static hazard — keep it out or fix it
Static control — in place before handling

items.label: Nylon windbreaker worn over your coveralls
items.why: Synthetic outer layers are strong triboelectric generators — thousands of volts from ordinary movement. Outer garments are cotton or an approved static-dissipative fabric.

items.label: Pulling your sweater off at the workbench
items.why: Garment removal is one of the best static generators there is. Layers change outside the explosive area — never at the bench, and then you re-verify your grounds.

items.label: Bubble wrap and poly bags stored beside the device
items.why: Plastics are charge generators that cannot be effectively grounded — picking up a poly bag can generate around 20,000 V in dry air. They stay out of the cell.

items.label: Metal tray sitting on a rubber mat next to the initiator
items.why: That is a floating conductor: insulated from ground, it stores charge and can deliver all of it as a single spark. Bond it into the ground system or remove it.

items.label: Wrist strap with its 1-megohm series resistor, tested today
items.why: It drains your body charge continuously, and the resistor limits the current through you if you contact energized equipment. Daily testing is what makes it a control instead of a hope.

items.label: Shorting clip across the initiator's twisted lead wires
items.why: The shunt holds both leads at the same potential, so no discharge can drive current through the bridgewire. It stays on until a written procedure step removes it.

items.label: Bonding jumper clamped on before the container is opened
items.why: Bonding first brings the container, bench, and device to one potential before anything is touched or transferred — no gap left for a spark to jump.

items.label: Conductive flooring with daily-tested conductive footwear
items.why: Floor plus footwear is your hands-free drain path, keeping you at ground potential as you move. Untested footwear is just street shoes.

checkQuestions.question: Why is static electricity treated as a lethal ignition source around the initiators and detonators submitted for neutron radiography?
options: A discharge too small for a person to feel can still exceed the ignition energy of sensitive primary explosives and electroexplosive devices
Static charge concentrates the neutron beam onto the explosive fill
Static discharge matters only because it can fog the imaging system
The human body cannot store enough charge to matter, but machinery can
checkQuestions.explanation: Most people feel nothing below about 3,000 volts, yet a discharge near that threshold carries on the order of a millijoule or more — while the most sensitive primary explosives and bridgewire devices can be initiated by fractions of a millijoule. Perception is not protection.

checkQuestions.question: What is the difference between bonding and grounding?
options: Bonding connects a system to earth; grounding connects objects to each other
They are two words for the same connection
Bonding connects conductive objects to each other to eliminate potential differences between them; grounding connects the bonded system to earth so charge drains away
Bonding applies only to liquids and powders; grounding applies only to people
checkQuestions.explanation: Bonded objects may still carry charge, but they are charged together, so no spark can jump between them; grounding then drains the whole bonded system to earth potential. The two connections do different jobs, and a safe cell uses both.

checkQuestions.question: A personnel wrist strap for explosive-device work includes a series resistance of about 1 megohm. Its purpose is to:
options: Speed up the drain so the body discharges instantly to zero
Limit the current through the wearer during accidental contact with energized equipment while still bleeding static charge away
Generate a small opposing charge that neutralizes the body
Insulate the wearer completely from the bench ground
checkQuestions.explanation: Static still drains through 1 megohm in a fraction of a second, but a dead-short copper path would place your body directly across any electrical fault you touched. The resistor makes the strap safe for you while keeping it effective against static.

checkQuestions.question: Which combination satisfies typical clothing requirements for explosive-device radiography?
options: Polyester coveralls with latex gloves, since both are disposable
Cotton coveralls with a nylon windbreaker added for cold weather
Cotton coveralls and conductive shoes, removing your sweater at the bench when you warm up
Cotton outer coveralls and daily-tested conductive footwear, with any layer changes made outside the explosive area
checkQuestions.explanation: Synthetics are prohibited as outer layers, garment removal inside the area is itself a high-voltage static generator, and footwear counts as a control only when it is tested daily with the floor system.

checkQuestions.question: An initiator arrives with its lead wires twisted together and a shorting clip installed. The clip's function is to:
options: Carry the firing current during the radiographic exposure
Hold both lead wires at the same potential so a stray discharge cannot drive current through the bridgewire
Mark the device as inert and safe for unrestricted handling
Connect the device's case to the earth electrode
checkQuestions.explanation: Current flows through the bridgewire only if a potential difference exists between the leads; the shunt erases that difference. It is not a ground connection and certainly not an inert marker — it stays installed until a written procedure step removes it.

references: CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §6.0–6.3 (NR explosive-device safety: static electricity, grounding devices, clothing requirements)
NFPA 77, Recommended Practice on Static Electricity (bonding, grounding, humidification, and personnel grounding)
DESR 6055.09, DoD Defense Explosives Safety Regulation — cardinal principle and explosive-area controls, described generically; your facility SOP governs
DOD-HDBK-263 Electrostatic Discharge Control Handbook (typical triboelectric voltages and human-body capacitance)
